title: Whether municipal libraries should compete with cafés for third-place attention
summary: Argue whether public libraries should redesign themselves around comfort and laptop work to compete with cafés as the city's 'third place', or hold a quieter ground that cafés cannot offer.
topic: Whether municipal libraries should redesign themselves as third places to compete with cafés
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether municipal libraries should follow the trend of redesigning their reading rooms with armchairs, coffee bars, and outlet-rich co-working desks to compete with cafés as the city's 'third place', or whether they should hold a quieter, less commercial ground that cafés structurally cannot offer. Anchor in a real city — Lisbon, Belgrade, Antwerp, Tashkent, or one you know. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted,'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Take a clear position. Avoid romanticising silence for its own sake.
hintStarters: Visiting Lisbon's main municipal library on a rainy Tuesday, you notice that the new ground-floor renovation has installed an espresso bar, soft armchairs, and outlets every metre, and that nobody is reading…
What a library can offer that a café structurally cannot is something library administrators have begun to forget in their understandable rush to…
Granted, the empty library is a dead one, and the romance of the silent hall stocked with rare manuscripts has very little to do with the daily life of…

hintVocab.meaningVi: nơi thứ ba (không phải nhà cũng không phải nơi làm việc)
tái thiết kế xung quanh sự thoải mái

hintVocab.phrase: rate of return on civic investment
hintVocab.meaningVi: tỷ lệ hoàn vốn đầu tư dân sự
chạy theo số lượng người vào
nhìn chằm chằm vào màn hình
làm loãng một lợi ích công cộng
có hình dáng của quán cà phê
nhầm sự thoải mái với sự hữu ích
từ bỏ giá trị đặc trưng của mình

sampleText:
Visiting Lisbon's main municipal library on a rainy Tuesday, you notice that the new ground-floor renovation has installed an espresso bar, soft armchairs, and outlets every metre, and that nobody is reading a book. Forty laptops are open. Two students are arguing over a shared spreadsheet. The bookshelves, retreated to the back wall, look at the room as if they had been demoted. The renovation has been a success on the metric the renovation was designed to win: footfall is up forty per cent. On the metric the building was originally for, something quieter has been lost.

What a library can offer that a café structurally cannot is something library administrators have begun to forget in their understandable rush to chase footfall. A café requires a transaction. To stay, you must order. To stay longer, you must reorder. The clock that runs above every café table is an invisible meter — and a person who cannot afford to keep feeding it is asked, gently or otherwise, to leave. A library has no such clock. It is the last large indoor room in a city centre where someone is allowed to sit for six hours, ask for nothing, and not be regarded as a problem.

Granted, the empty library is a dead one, and the romance of the silent hall stocked with rare manuscripts has very little to do with the daily life of a working public branch. Comfort is not the enemy. A library that wants to be used has to admit chairs people can actually sit in for an hour, lighting people do not have to squint under, and a children's section the parents do not have to apologise for. Public libraries did not become inviting by accident, and the librarians who fought for the warmer renovations had reasons that had nothing to do with cafés.

Even so, the comfort that a library offers should be the comfort that a café cannot offer, not a worse version of the same comfort. What a café cannot offer is the right to loiter without consuming, the right to read for free, the right to print a CV when one cannot print at home, the right to occupy public space without performing the role of customer. The minute the library installs the same furniture and adds the same coffee bar, it is competing on terrain where the café will always win — and abandoning the terrain where it has no competitor.

The library that wins the third-place fight by becoming a free café has, by that victory, retired the one civic function nobody else in the city is willing to perform. A city that loses that function does not get it back by adding more cafés. It loses something for which cafés, however pleasant, were never an answer.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft naming the library's irreplaceable function
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a library can offer that a café structurally cannot is something library administrators have begun to forget' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' với một mệnh đề quan hệ định ngữ ('that a café structurally cannot') để giới hạn câu trả lời chính xác. Đây là cách tinh tế để loại bỏ các câu trả lời thay thế ngay trong câu hỏi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a library can offer that a café structurally cannot is something library administrators have begun to forget' uses a what-cleft with an embedded defining relative clause ('that a café structurally cannot') to narrow the answer precisely. It is a subtle way of ruling out alternative answers inside the question itself.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Participle clause for descriptive backgrounding
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The bookshelves, retreated to the back wall, look at the room as if they had been demoted' chèn một mệnh đề phân từ ('retreated to the back wall') giữa chủ ngữ và động từ chính để thêm chi tiết mô tả mà không cần một câu phụ. Cấu trúc này điển hình cho prose mô tả ở trình độ B2.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The bookshelves, retreated to the back wall, look at the room as if they had been demoted' inserts a past-participle clause ('retreated to the back wall') between subject and main verb to add descriptive detail without a full subordinate clause. The structure is typical of B2 descriptive prose.

sampleTranslationVi:
Đến thăm thư viện thành phố chính của Lisbon vào một thứ Ba mưa, bạn nhận ra rằng việc tân trang tầng trệt mới đã lắp đặt một quầy bar espresso, ghế bành mềm, và ổ cắm mỗi mét, và rằng không ai đang đọc sách. Bốn mươi máy tính xách tay đang mở. Hai sinh viên đang tranh luận về một bảng tính chia sẻ. Các kệ sách, rút lui về bức tường sau, nhìn căn phòng như thể chúng đã bị giáng chức. Việc tân trang đã thành công ở chỉ số mà nó được thiết kế để giành: lượng người vào tăng bốn mươi phần trăm. Ở chỉ số mà tòa nhà ban đầu được xây dựng cho, một thứ gì đó yên lặng hơn đã bị mất.

Điều mà một thư viện có thể cung cấp mà một quán cà phê về mặt cấu trúc không thể là điều mà các quản trị viên thư viện đã bắt đầu quên trong sự vội vã có thể hiểu được của họ để chạy theo lượng người vào. Một quán cà phê đòi hỏi một giao dịch. Để ở lại, bạn phải gọi món. Để ở lại lâu hơn, bạn phải gọi món lại. Cái đồng hồ chạy phía trên mỗi bàn cà phê là một đồng hồ đo vô hình — và một người không đủ khả năng tiếp tục nuôi nó được yêu cầu, một cách nhẹ nhàng hay không, phải rời đi. Một thư viện không có đồng hồ như vậy. Đó là phòng trong nhà lớn cuối cùng ở trung tâm thành phố nơi một người được phép ngồi sáu tiếng, không yêu cầu gì, và không bị coi là một vấn đề.

Công nhận, thư viện trống rỗng là một thư viện chết, và sự lãng mạn của hội trường yên lặng chứa đầy bản thảo quý hiếm có rất ít liên quan đến đời sống hàng ngày của một chi nhánh công cộng đang hoạt động. Sự thoải mái không phải là kẻ thù. Một thư viện muốn được sử dụng phải chấp nhận những chiếc ghế người ta thực sự có thể ngồi trong một giờ, ánh sáng người ta không phải nheo mắt dưới đó, và một khu trẻ em mà cha mẹ không phải xin lỗi cho. Các thư viện công cộng không trở nên mời gọi một cách ngẫu nhiên, và các thủ thư đã đấu tranh cho các đợt tân trang ấm áp hơn có những lý do không liên quan gì đến quán cà phê.

Dẫu vậy, sự thoải mái mà một thư viện cung cấp nên là sự thoải mái mà một quán cà phê không thể cung cấp, không phải là phiên bản kém hơn của cùng sự thoải mái đó. Điều mà một quán cà phê không thể cung cấp là quyền nán lại mà không tiêu thụ, quyền đọc miễn phí, quyền in CV khi không thể in ở nhà, quyền chiếm dụng không gian công cộng mà không phải đóng vai khách hàng. Phút mà thư viện lắp đặt cùng đồ đạc và thêm cùng một quầy cà phê, nó đang cạnh tranh trên địa hình mà quán cà phê sẽ luôn luôn thắng — và từ bỏ địa hình mà nó không có đối thủ cạnh tranh.

Thư viện thắng cuộc chiến nơi thứ ba bằng cách trở thành một quán cà phê miễn phí đã, qua chiến thắng đó, cho nghỉ hưu một chức năng dân sự mà không ai khác trong thành phố sẵn sàng thực hiện. Một thành phố mất chức năng đó không lấy lại được bằng cách thêm nhiều quán cà phê hơn. Nó mất một thứ mà quán cà phê, dù dễ chịu đến đâu, chưa bao giờ là câu trả lời.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft with an embedded relative clause to name the library's irreplaceable function?
options: What a library can offer that a café structurally cannot is something library administrators have begun to forget.
A library can offer what that a café cannot is structurally administrators have begun to forget something library.
Library administrators what have begun to forget that a café structurally cannot is offer a library something.
Structurally cannot library administrators have begun to forget is offer that a café what a library can something.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' contains the embedded relative 'that a café structurally cannot', which narrows the answer to exactly the irreplaceable function.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'third place' mean in this essay?
options: The third-best place in a city after home and work
A neutral, accessible social space outside the home (first place) and the workplace (second place) — historically the café, the pub, the library
A place that finishes third in a competition
A place located on the third floor of a building
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'third place' is a sociological concept naming the public or semi-public spaces where civic life happens — and libraries and cafés have long competed for that role.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of the first two places — home and work — and then the next category.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Granted,' as a concession opener?
options: Granted, the empty library is a dead one, and the romance of the silent hall stocked with rare manuscripts has very little to do with the daily life of a working public branch.
The empty library granted is a dead one stocked with the romance of the silent hall and rare manuscripts has very little to do with daily life a working public branch.
Granted little to do has the empty library is a dead one with daily life of working public branch the romance the silent hall.
Romance of the silent hall granted the empty library a dead one is and rare manuscripts has stocked very little to do.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause is the cleanest B2 concession opener.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction backgrounds the librarians while putting the building in focus?
options: Something quieter has been lost on the metric the building was originally for.
Has been lost on the metric something quieter the building for was originally.
The building was originally for something quieter has been lost on the metric.
On the metric something quieter the building was originally lost has been for.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'has been lost' and 'was originally for' are passive constructions that put the loss and the building in subject position, hiding the agents (the renovators, the council).
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'has been lost' and 'was originally for' to background the actors?

options: To pursue people physically through a building
To redesign or programme a public space primarily to maximise the number of visitors counted at the door
To run after someone on foot
To install footstep sensors in the floor
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Footfall' is the count of people entering a space. Chasing it as a goal can lead institutions to optimise for the number rather than for what the visitors actually do.
mcQuestions.hint: What is 'footfall' as a business metric, and what does 'chase' suggest?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a participle clause to set the analytical scene?
options: Visiting Lisbon's main municipal library on a rainy Tuesday, you notice that the new ground-floor renovation has installed an espresso bar.
You notice visiting Lisbon's main municipal library on a rainy Tuesday that the new ground-floor renovation has installed an espresso bar.
An espresso bar has installed Lisbon's main municipal library on a rainy Tuesday visiting you notice the new ground-floor renovation.
The new ground-floor renovation on a rainy Tuesday visiting Lisbon's main municipal library has installed an espresso bar you notice.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Visiting Lisbon's main municipal library on a rainy Tuesday' is a present-participle clause opener that sets the analytical scene before the main clause delivers the observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example, all libraries should serve coffee.
Even so, the comfort that a library offers should be the comfort that a café cannot offer, not a worse version of the same comfort.
On the contrary, no library has ever been comfortable.
Furthermore, all libraries are perfect.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (comfort is not the enemy) and pivots back to the writer's main argument (the comfort must be a different kind).
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's broader position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the right to loiter without consuming' mean?
options: The legal right to commit minor crimes in public
The right to occupy a public space without being required to buy anything in order to remain
The right to enter shops without buying
The right to consume only when one wants to
mcQuestions.explanation: Loitering without consumption is the civic right that distinguishes a library from a café — staying without the obligation to spend.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'loiter' mean, and what would 'without consuming' add?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly, libraries trying to be cafés is just kind of cringe, no question.
It's kind of obvious that libraries should just stay quiet and bookish, full stop.
The conversion of municipal reading rooms into outlet-rich, comfort-optimised co-working environments represents an institutional convergence with private commercial space.
Libraries are like, you know, supposed to be quiet places and stuff.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('institutional convergence', 'comfort-optimised') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All public libraries should be replaced with cafés that serve free coffee.
Libraries should never install any comfortable furniture or modern amenities.

Libraries can and should be comfortable, but the comfort they offer should be the kind a café structurally cannot — free, unconditional, consumption-free occupation of civic space — rather than a worse imitation of café comfort.
Libraries should compete head-to-head with cafés on coffee quality.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay rejects both the silent-hall romance and the café imitation, arguing for comfort that exploits what only libraries can offer.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraphs four and five for the writer's specific verdict.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'It is the last large indoor room in a city centre where someone is allowed to sit for six hours, ask for nothing, and not be regarded as a problem'?
options: It is the most crowded indoor space available in the city centre.
It is one of the only large interior public spaces left in the city where prolonged presence without spending money is socially acceptable.
It is the largest building in the city.
It is the only building that requires no membership card.
mcQuestions.explanation: The sentence names the rare civic right libraries protect — extended unconditional presence in central indoor space, without the social pressure of being a non-customer.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'sit for six hours, ask for nothing, and not be regarded as a problem' actually permit?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to mistake comfort for usefulness' mean?
options: To use a chair you find comfortable as a tool
To assume that because a space is comfortable, it must also be doing useful civic work — when comfort alone can disguise the loss of an institution's distinctive function
To assume that comfortable institutions never close
To rate institutions only by their seating
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay warns that comfort is easy to measure and easy to celebrate, but comfort by itself does not prove that the institution is still doing the public work it was built for.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the danger of celebrating comfort if the underlying function has been lost?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a defining relative clause correctly?
options: The library that wins the third-place fight by becoming a free café has, by that victory, retired the one civic function nobody else in the city is willing to perform.
Wins the third-place fight by becoming a free café the library that has, by that victory, retired the one civic function nobody else.
The one civic function nobody else in the city is willing to perform the library that wins the third-place fight by becoming a free café has retired by that victory.
By that victory the library has retired wins the third-place fight by becoming a free café the one civic function nobody else.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'that wins the third-place fight by becoming a free café' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which library the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to dilute a public good' mean?
options: To add water to a public swimming pool
To weaken the distinctive civic value of a public institution by spreading its mission across functions that private alternatives already perform
To reduce the number of customers a public service has
To allow public goods to expire
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Dilute' means weaken by mixing in. The essay argues that when a library becomes a free café, it dilutes the specific public good only it can provide.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'dilute' do — and what does a 'public good' lose when its distinctive function is blurred?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence closes the essay with a sharp final verdict?
options: In conclusion, both cafés and libraries are useful in different ways.
Therefore, all cafés must be replaced with libraries.
It loses something for which cafés, however pleasant, were never an answer.
To sum up, libraries should consider all options.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a sharp summary verdict — pleasant cafés cannot substitute for the civic function libraries uniquely perform.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a memorable verdict on what cafés cannot replace?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what is the strongest evidence that a library renovation has gone wrong?
options: When footfall drops below the previous year's level
When the renovation succeeds on the metric it was designed for (footfall) while the civic function it was built for — free, unconditional, consumption-free use — is quietly lost
When the coffee bar generates too much revenue
When the bookshelves are moved closer to the entrance
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph one names this exact paradox: success on the new metric, loss on the original one.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph one for the writer's two-metric diagnosis.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a public library in a city you know. What can it currently offer that no café in the same neighbourhood can? If the budget for a renovation arrived tomorrow, what would you spend it on first to protect that distinctive function, and what would you refuse to spend it on, even if it would lift footfall?
